sources.title: EU AI Act takes effect, companies scramble to comply

claims.claim: Over 60% of EU-based AI companies have not completed compliance assessments
claims.verification_note: Cited as industry survey but original survey not publicly available

sources.title: 欧盟人工智能法案正式生效

claims.claim: Chinese AI companies operating in the EU have already achieved full compliance
claims.verification_note: Contradicted by European Digital Rights report (src-003) documenting ongoing non-compliance cases

sources.title: AI Act Compliance Tracker: Month One

perspectives.position: The AI Act provides necessary consumer protection and sets a global regulatory standard
The regulation is overly burdensome and will push AI innovation outside the EU
The AI Act represents European regulatory overreach that fragments the global AI market

divergences.description: Xinhua reports Chinese companies have achieved full compliance; EDRi documents ongoing non-compliance cases involving Chinese-operated AI systems in the EU
divergences.resolution_note: Independent verification pending. Both sources may define 'compliance' differently.

gaps.description: No sources from African, South American, or South Asian perspectives on the global impact of EU AI regulation
No voices from workers whose jobs are directly affected by AI systems now under regulation

article.headline: EU AI Act Enforcement Begins Amid Compliance Questions
article.subheadline: As Europe's landmark AI regulation takes effect, the gap between policy ambition and industry readiness becomes visible
article.body: [Article body would be here — source-based, multi-perspective, with inline citations like [src-001]]
article.summary: The EU AI Act officially took effect this week, but compliance data tells conflicting stories. European regulators claim progress while industry groups warn of innovation flight. Chinese state media asserts full compliance by domestic firms — a claim disputed by European digital rights organizations.

examples: 'scramble to comply' in headline implies negative judgment of companies

findings: Topic framed primarily as EU-vs-industry tension
Alternative framing as global governance question underrepresented
Focus on corporate compliance over societal impact
omitted_contexts: Historical context of EU regulatory approach
Comparison with other jurisdictions' AI policies

findings: Chinese perspective present but only via state media

transparency.selection_reason: High-impact regulatory event affecting global AI industry. Multiple conflicting narratives across language boundaries.
discarded_topics: SpaceX Starship test flight (well-covered, single narrative)
G7 trade meeting (no significant divergences found)

revisions.action: Flagged src-002 claim as disputed, added counter-evidence reference
Marked geographical bias as 0.5 — missing Global South perspectives
